---
title: "Chapter 24 — 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 23 · 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations](ch23.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [25 · 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning →](ch25.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Model-free reinforcement learning is how an agent learns to act well in an environment it does **not** understand — it has no map of how states transition or where rewards come from, only the experience it gathers by trying things. Instead of planning against a known model (as in Chapter 23's dynamic programming), the agent estimates value directly from sampled interaction and slowly bends its behavior toward what pays off. This family — Monte Carlo, temporal-difference, Q-learning, SARSA — is the practical core of RL and the launching point for everything in deep RL.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Reinforcement Learning · learning to act from sampled experience without knowing the environment's dynamics · estimate values by sampling, then act greedily with respect to them while still exploring
:::

## 24.1 — Learning without a model

In Chapter 23 the agent had the rules of the game: it knew the transition probabilities $P(s'\mid s,a)$ and the reward function $R(s,a)$, so it could compute the best policy by reasoning, like a chess engine that knows every legal move. Model-free RL throws that luxury away. The agent is dropped into a world where it can only *do* things and *see* what happens — it presses buttons, observes the next state and a reward, and must infer good behavior from that stream alone.

The quantity it wants is the **value** of acting. The **action-value function** $Q^\pi(s,a)$ is the expected total discounted reward from taking action $a$ in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$ thereafter:

$$Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t R_{t} \,\middle|\, S_0 = s,\, A_0 = a\right]$$

where $\gamma \in [0,1)$ is the **discount factor** that makes near rewards worth more than distant ones. If you knew $Q^*(s,a)$ — the value under the *best* policy — acting optimally would be trivial: in each state pick the action with the largest $Q$. Model-free RL is, at heart, a set of recipes for **estimating $Q$ from samples** and improving the policy as those estimates sharpen.

Two ideas power everything that follows. **Sampling**: since you cannot average over all possible futures (you don't know their probabilities), you average over the futures you actually experienced. **Bootstrapping**: rather than wait for a full future to play out, you can update an estimate using your *current* estimate of what comes next — guessing from a guess, then correcting. Monte Carlo uses sampling without bootstrapping; temporal-difference uses both. That single distinction organizes the whole chapter.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Environment unknown] --> B{Use a model?}
  B -- No, sample full episodes --> C[Monte Carlo]
  B -- No, sample + bootstrap --> D[Temporal-Difference]
  D --> E[SARSA on-policy]
  D --> F[Q-learning off-policy]
```

## 24.2 — Monte Carlo: learn from complete episodes

The simplest honest way to estimate "how good is this state-action?" is to try it many times and average what you got. That is **Monte Carlo (MC)** learning: play an episode all the way to the end, compute the actual **return** $G_t$ — the real discounted sum of rewards that followed — and use it as a sample of the value.

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots$$

After many episodes, the average of the observed returns from a state-action pair converges to its true value, exactly the way flipping a coin many times reveals its bias. Concretely, MC keeps a running mean. Each time you visit $(s,a)$ and later observe return $G$, you nudge the estimate toward it:

$$Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\,\big[G - Q(s,a)\big]$$

with $\alpha$ a step size (use $\alpha = 1/N$ for the exact running average, or a small constant to track a changing target).

A practical subtlety: within a single episode you might visit the same $(s,a)$ more than once. **First-visit MC** averages only the return following the *first* time a pair is visited in each episode; **every-visit MC** averages over all visits. Both converge to $Q^\pi$; first-visit is the cleaner one to reason about because its samples are independent across episodes.

The appeal is honesty: MC uses *real* returns, so it is **unbiased** — it makes no assumption about the environment. The catch is twofold. First, you must **wait for the episode to end** before you can learn anything, which rules out continuing tasks with no natural finish. Second, real returns are **noisy**: a single episode's outcome depends on a long chain of random transitions and choices, so MC estimates have **high variance** and converge slowly. Think of grading a student only by final exam scores — fair, but it takes many exams to see their true ability, and you learn nothing mid-course.

::: {.callout-tip}
MC's mantra: **don't guess, just average what actually happened.** It is the right baseline when episodes are short and you distrust any model of the dynamics.
:::

## 24.3 — Temporal-Difference learning: bootstrap from each step

Monte Carlo waits until the end. **Temporal-difference (TD)** learning refuses to wait — it updates after *every single step* by bootstrapping, replacing the unknown rest-of-the-future with its own current estimate of it.

The intuition is the commuter's: each morning you predict your total drive time. Halfway in, you hit unexpected traffic. You don't wait until you arrive to learn — you immediately revise today's estimate using what you've seen so far *plus* your standing guess for the remaining leg. TD does exactly this. The simplest version, **TD(0)**, updates the value of a state using one observed reward and the estimated value of the next state:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha\,\big[\underbrace{R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})}_{\text{TD target}} - V(S_t)\big]$$

The bracket is the **TD error** $\delta_t$ — the gap between your one-step-improved estimate (the **TD target**) and your old one. You shrink it a little each step.

This buys two things over MC. You learn **online**, before the episode ends, so TD works in continuing tasks. And because the target $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ depends on just one random reward and transition rather than a whole episode's worth, TD has **much lower variance**. The price is **bias**: $V(S_{t+1})$ is only a guess, so early on you are bootstrapping from wrong numbers. In practice the variance reduction usually wins, and TD methods dominate.

| | Monte Carlo | TD(0) |
|---|---|---|
| Updates after | a full episode | every step |
| Target | actual return $G_t$ | $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ |
| Bias / variance | unbiased / high variance | biased / low variance |
| Continuing tasks? | no | yes |
| Bootstraps? | no | yes |

**TD(λ)** interpolates between the two extremes. Instead of looking one step ahead (TD) or all the way to the end (MC), it blends **$n$-step returns** for all $n$ at once, weighted by $\lambda \in [0,1]$: $\lambda=0$ recovers TD(0), $\lambda=1$ recovers Monte Carlo, and values in between often learn fastest. It is implemented efficiently with **eligibility traces** — a short-term memory that marks recently visited states as "eligible" for credit, so when a TD error appears it is distributed backward across the states that led there, not just the immediate one.

## 24.4 — Q-learning vs SARSA: off-policy and on-policy control

Estimating values is half the job; the other half is **control** — using those estimates to improve behavior. Both leading TD control algorithms learn $Q(s,a)$ and act greedily (mostly) with respect to it, but they differ in one subtle, important way: *which next action they assume when forming the TD target.*

**SARSA** is **on-policy**: it learns the value of the policy it is actually following, including its exploratory mistakes. Its name is its update — it uses the tuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$, where $A_{t+1}$ is the action the agent *actually* takes next:

$$Q(S_t,A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha\big[R_{t+1} + \gamma\,Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t,A_t)\big]$$

**Q-learning** is **off-policy**: it learns the value of the *optimal* policy regardless of how the agent is currently behaving. Its target uses the **best** next action — the $\max$ over $a'$ — not the one actually taken:

$$Q(S_t,A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha\big[R_{t+1} + \gamma\,\max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t,A_t)\big]$$

The difference looks tiny — $Q(S_{t+1},A_{t+1})$ versus $\max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1},a')$ — but it changes the personality of the agent. The textbook illustration is the **cliff-walking** gridworld: a row of states along the edge of a cliff, where the shortest path hugs the edge but one exploratory misstep plunges off it for a big penalty. Q-learning learns the optimal edge-hugging path (it assumes it will act greedily later, ignoring its own exploration), so it scores higher *if* exploration is eventually turned off — but takes more falls while learning. SARSA, accounting for the fact that it *will* sometimes explore, learns a safer path one step away from the edge.

- **On-policy (SARSA):** evaluates and improves the policy being used; safer under persistent exploration; converges to a near-optimal policy that respects its own randomness.
- **Off-policy (Q-learning):** learns the greedy optimal policy from data generated by a different (exploratory) behavior policy; more sample-flexible (can learn from replayed or others' experience), which is exactly why it underpins Deep Q-Networks in Chapter 25.

::: {.callout-warning}
A common confusion: Q-learning is off-policy **not** because it doesn't explore — it still explores to collect data — but because its *update target* ignores the exploratory action and uses the greedy $\max$ instead. The behavior that gathers data and the policy being valued are two different things.
:::

## 24.5 — Exploration: the epsilon-greedy compromise

If the agent always takes the action its current $Q$ says is best, it can lock onto a mediocre habit and never discover something better — like always ordering the one dish you know at a restaurant, never learning the chef's special is amazing. But if it explores too much, it wastes reward on bad actions. This is the **exploration-exploitation tradeoff**, and the simplest workable answer is **epsilon-greedy**.

With probability $1-\varepsilon$ take the **greedy** action $\arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ (exploit); with probability $\varepsilon$ take a uniformly random action (explore):

$$
\pi(a\mid s) =
\begin{cases}
1-\varepsilon + \varepsilon/|\mathcal{A}| & a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s,a')\\[4pt]
\varepsilon/|\mathcal{A}| & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

A typical recipe starts with $\varepsilon$ high (say 1.0, pure exploration) and **decays** it toward a small floor as learning proceeds, so the agent explores aggressively early when its estimates are bad and exploits later when they are trustworthy. Crisp, parameter-light, and good enough for the tabular methods above — its weakness is that it explores *blindly*, spending equal effort on plainly terrible actions and genuinely uncertain ones. Section 24.7 shows smarter alternatives for the special case of bandits.

There is one theoretical caveat worth naming. For these algorithms to provably converge, every state-action pair must keep getting visited — a condition called **exploration in the limit**. Keeping $\varepsilon$ strictly positive guarantees it; decaying it too fast can starve some pairs of data and stall learning. The classic safe schedule (GLIE: greedy in the limit with infinite exploration) decays $\varepsilon$ slowly enough that exploration never fully stops too early, yet the policy still becomes greedy in the end.

## 24.6 — A working Q-learning update

The whole tabular Q-learning loop fits in a few lines: keep a table $Q[s,a]$, act epsilon-greedily, and apply the off-policy update after each transition. The mechanism is the entire method — no model, no episode buffer, just one correction per step.

```python
import numpy as np

def q_learning(env, n_states, n_actions,
               alpha=0.1, gamma=0.99, eps=0.1, episodes=500):
    Q = np.zeros((n_states, n_actions))          # value table, optimistic-free init
    for _ in range(episodes):
        s = env.reset()
        done = False
        while not done:
            # epsilon-greedy action selection
            if np.random.rand() < eps:
                a = np.random.randint(n_actions)  # explore
            else:
                a = int(np.argmax(Q[s]))          # exploit
            s2, r, done = env.step(a)
            # off-policy TD target uses max over next actions
            target = r + gamma * np.max(Q[s2]) * (not done)
            Q[s, a] += alpha * (target - Q[s, a]) # shrink the TD error
            s = s2
    return Q
```

Two details carry weight. The `(not done)` factor zeroes the bootstrap at terminal states — there is no future to discount, so the target is just the final reward; forgetting this is the most common tabular bug. And turning this into **SARSA** means one change: pick $a'$ for the next state with the *same* epsilon-greedy rule and use $Q[s2, a']$ in the target instead of $\max Q[s2]$.

## 24.7 — Multi-armed bandits: exploration in its purest form

Strip away the state transitions entirely and you get the **multi-armed bandit**: a row of slot machines (one-armed bandits), each paying out from an unknown fixed distribution. There is one state; every round you pull one arm and get a reward. With no dynamics to worry about, the *only* problem left is exploration versus exploitation — which makes bandits the cleanest lab for studying it, and a workhorse in their own right for A/B testing, ad selection, and clinical trials.

The performance measure is **regret**: how much total reward you lost versus always pulling the truly best arm. Good algorithms drive regret to grow only *logarithmically* with the number of pulls. Epsilon-greedy works but is wasteful (it keeps sampling bad arms at a fixed rate forever); two smarter strategies dominate.

**Upper Confidence Bound (UCB)** embodies "optimism in the face of uncertainty." For each arm it keeps the average reward $\hat{\mu}_i$ plus an exploration bonus that is large when the arm has been tried few times, then pulls whichever arm has the highest optimistic estimate:

$$a_t = \arg\max_i \left[\hat{\mu}_i + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_i}}\right]$$

where $N_i$ is the number of pulls of arm $i$ and $t$ the round. Rarely-pulled arms get a fat bonus and are tried; as $N_i$ grows the bonus shrinks and UCB settles on the best arm. Unlike epsilon-greedy, it explores *deliberately* — directing attention to the arms it is genuinely unsure about.

**Thompson sampling** takes a Bayesian route. Keep a probability distribution over each arm's true reward (for example a Beta distribution over a click-through rate). Each round, **sample** one plausible value from each arm's distribution, pull the arm with the highest sample, then update that arm's distribution with the observed reward. Arms whose distributions are wide (uncertain) occasionally produce high samples and get explored; arms that are confidently good get pulled often. It is simple, remarkably effective in practice, and naturally extends to many real systems.

The geometry of "optimism" is easy to picture: each arm has an estimate with an uncertainty interval around it, and UCB ranks arms by the *top* of that interval, not its center.

<svg viewBox="0 0 360 170" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11">
  <line x1="30" y1="140" x2="340" y2="140" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <!-- arm A: high mean, narrow -->
  <line x1="80" y1="70" x2="80" y2="110" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="72" y1="70" x2="88" y2="70" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="72" y1="110" x2="88" y2="110" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="80" cy="90" r="3" fill="currentColor"/>
  <text x="80" y="155" text-anchor="middle">arm A</text>
  <text x="80" y="62" text-anchor="middle">UCB</text>
  <!-- arm B: lower mean, wide (more uncertain) -> higher upper bound -->
  <line x1="200" y1="40" x2="200" y2="120" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="3 3"/>
  <line x1="192" y1="40" x2="208" y2="40" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="192" y1="120" x2="208" y2="120" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="200" cy="100" r="3" fill="currentColor"/>
  <text x="200" y="155" text-anchor="middle">arm B</text>
  <text x="200" y="32" text-anchor="middle">UCB (wins)</text>
  <text x="250" y="100" font-size="10">B's mean is lower,</text>
  <text x="250" y="114" font-size="10">but its top is higher,</text>
  <text x="250" y="128" font-size="10">so UCB tries it.</text>
</svg>

| Strategy | How it explores | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| ε-greedy | fixed random fraction | trivial to implement | explores blindly, never stops |
| UCB | optimism bonus by uncertainty | strong logarithmic-regret guarantees | assumes stationary rewards |
| Thompson | sampling from posterior | excellent empirically, Bayesian | needs a posterior model |

## 24.8 — Why tables don't scale, and what comes next

Everything so far assumed a **tabular** setting: a literal table with one cell per state-action pair. That is fine for a small gridworld, but it collapses on real problems. Backgammon has roughly $10^{20}$ states; a video game frame of raw pixels has astronomically more; any continuous state (a robot's joint angles, a car's velocity) has *infinitely* many. You can neither store a cell for each nor visit each often enough to average — the table is mostly empty forever. This is the **curse of dimensionality**.

The deeper problem is that tables have **no notion of similarity**. Two nearly identical screens are, to a table, completely unrelated entries; learning about one teaches nothing about the other. Yet generalization is the whole point of intelligence — we expect that what we learned in one situation transfers to similar ones.

The fix is **function approximation**: replace the table $Q(s,a)$ with a parameterized function $\hat{Q}(s,a;\theta)$ — a linear model over features, or a neural network — that *generalizes* across states. Now a handful of weights $\theta$ summarize values over an enormous or continuous space, and updating from one experience automatically adjusts the estimate for all similar states. The TD update becomes a gradient step that nudges $\theta$ to reduce the TD error. Marrying this idea with deep neural networks gives **Deep Q-Networks** and the rest of deep RL — the subject of Chapter 25. The algorithms in this chapter don't get discarded; they become the learning *targets* that the network is trained to predict.

## 24.x — Key takeaways

- **Model-free RL** learns to act from sampled experience alone, with no knowledge of transition or reward dynamics; it estimates value functions and acts greedily (with exploration) toward them.
- **Monte Carlo** averages complete-episode returns: unbiased but high-variance, and needs episodes to terminate. First-visit and every-visit variants both converge.
- **Temporal-difference** learning bootstraps — it updates each step from $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$: lower variance, works online and in continuing tasks, at the cost of some bias. **TD(λ)** with eligibility traces spans the MC–TD spectrum.
- **SARSA** (on-policy) values the policy it actually follows; **Q-learning** (off-policy) uses the $\max$ over next actions to learn the optimal policy from any behavior — the only update difference is $Q(s',a')$ vs $\max_{a'}Q(s',a')$.
- **Epsilon-greedy** is the default exploration rule: mostly greedy, occasionally random, with $\varepsilon$ usually decayed over time but kept positive long enough to keep visiting every state-action pair.
- **Bandits** isolate pure exploration; **UCB** (optimism via confidence bonuses) and **Thompson sampling** (posterior sampling) beat blind epsilon-greedy and achieve logarithmic regret.
- **Tabular methods don't scale** — the curse of dimensionality and lack of generalization across states motivate **function approximation** and deep RL.

## 24.y — See also

- **Chapter 23 — Reinforcement Learning Foundations** — MDPs, returns, the Bellman equations, and the model-based dynamic-programming methods this chapter does without.
- **Chapter 25 — Deep Reinforcement Learning** — function approximation with neural networks, Deep Q-Networks, and policy-gradient methods that scale these ideas.
- **Chapter 26 — Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory** — what happens when several learning agents share an environment.
- **Chapter 12 — Bayesian Machine Learning** — the posterior reasoning behind Thompson sampling.
- **Chapter 14 — Neural Network Fundamentals** & **Chapter 15 — Training Deep Neural Networks** — the approximators and gradient methods that replace the Q-table.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 23 · 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations](ch23.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [25 · 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning →](ch25.html)
